content_of_post_to_answer: Why do we get so used to conveniences like multiple bathrooms, and how does it affect us when we don't have them?
answer: The interesting thing about conveniences like multiple bathrooms is that we don't just get used to the object itself; we get used to the expectation of uninterrupted control over our time and space. A second bathroom quietly removes tiny negotiations: waiting, adjusting schedules, sharing discomfort. When it disappears, the inconvenience feels larger than the actual problem because we are not only losing a facility, we are losing a habit of independence. Many modern comforts work this way: they become invisible when present, but their absence reveals how much of our daily life was built around them.
